does: This paper introduces SeePhys Pro, a benchmark that systematically evaluates how well models maintain physics reasoning capabilities when critical information shifts from text to visual representations. The benchmark contains four semantically equivalent variants of physics problems with progressively increasing visual content, revealing that state-of-the-art models show significant performance degradation as information transfers from language to diagrams. The work identifies visual variable grounding as the primary bottleneck and demonstrates that models are not representation-invariant reasoners. Additionally, the authors conduct large-scale multimodal reinforcement learning from visual reasoning (RLVR) experiments with blind training—where all training images are masked—discovering that this counter-intuitive setup still improves performance on unmasked test sets, suggesting the gains come from textual artifacts and distributional biases rather than genuine multimodal learning.
connects: This work builds on the broader literature examining multimodal reasoning in language and vision models, extending prior research on vision-language understanding that has questioned whether models truly leverage visual information or rely on spurious correlations. It connects to benchmarking studies that probe representation invariance and semantic equivalence across modalities, as well as work on reinforcement learning from human feedback and RLVR that attempts to improve reasoning through training signals. The finding of blind-training effects relates to recent investigations into what language models actually learn during training, particularly work on hidden biases and shortcut learning. The emphasis on systematic ablation and diagnostic controls follows methodological practices established in mechanistic interpretability and model auditing research.
enables: This work opens avenues for developing truly multimodal physics reasoners that robustly handle information across representational forms. It motivates research into better visual grounding mechanisms and representation-invariant architectures that don't degrade when encountering diagrams rather than text descriptions. The blind-training discovery points toward improved training protocols that can detect and mitigate reliance on spurious cues, potentially leading to more robust multimodal models. The benchmark itself enables future work to measure progress toward representation-invariant reasoning and to develop diagnostic tools for identifying where multimodal systems fail. This framework could inspire similar progressive-transfer benchmarks in other domains, establishing a new standard for evaluating genuine multimodal understanding versus surface-level pattern matching.
